**Ingeborg Bachmann** (Austrian German: [[ˈɪŋəbɔrɡ ˈbaxman]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German"); 25 June 1926 – 17 October 1973) was an Austrian poet and author. She is regarded as one of the major voices of [German-language literature](/wiki/German_literature "German literature") in the 20th century. In [1963](/wiki/1963_Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "1963 Nobel Prize in Literature"), she was nominated for the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature") by German philologist Harald Patzer.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Bachmann was born in [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt"), in the Austrian state of [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia "Carinthia"), the daughter of Olga (née Haas) and Matthias Bachmann, a schoolteacher. Her father was an early member of the [Austrian National Socialist Party](/wiki/Austrian_National_Socialism "Austrian National Socialism"). She had a sister, Isolde, and a brother, Heinz.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)

She studied philosophy, psychology, German [philology](/wiki/Philology "Philology"), and law at the universities of [Innsbruck](/wiki/University_of_Innsbruck "University of Innsbruck"), [Graz](/wiki/University_of_Graz "University of Graz"), and [Vienna](/wiki/University_of_Vienna "University of Vienna"). In 1949, she received her PhD from the University of Vienna with her dissertation titled "The Critical Reception of the Existential Philosophy of [Martin Heidegger](/wiki/Martin_Heidegger "Martin Heidegger")";[[5]](#cite_note-5) her thesis adviser was [Victor Kraft](/wiki/Victor_Kraft "Victor Kraft").[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/db/Palazzo_sacchetti.jpg/250px-Palazzo_sacchetti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Palazzo_sacchetti.jpg)

Ingeborg Bachmann's residence at [Palazzo Sacchetti](/wiki/Palazzo_Sacchetti "Palazzo Sacchetti"), [Via Giulia](/wiki/Via_Giulia "Via Giulia"), Rome

After graduating, Bachmann worked as a scriptwriter and editor at the [Allied](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Austria "Allied-occupied Austria") radio station *Rot-Weiss-Rot*, a job that enabled her to obtain an overview of contemporary literature and also supplied her with a decent income, making possible proper literary work. Her first [radio dramas](/wiki/Radio_drama "Radio drama") were published by the station. Her literary career was enhanced by contact with [Hans Weigel](/wiki/Hans_Weigel "Hans Weigel") (littérateur and sponsor of young post-war literature) and the literary circle known as [Gruppe 47](/wiki/Group_47 "Group 47"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) whose members also included [Ilse Aichinger](/wiki/Ilse_Aichinger "Ilse Aichinger"), [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan"), [Heinrich Böll](/wiki/Heinrich_B%C3%B6ll "Heinrich Böll"), [Marcel Reich-Ranicki](/wiki/Marcel_Reich-Ranicki "Marcel Reich-Ranicki") and [Günter Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "Günter Grass").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

In 1953, she moved to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), Italy, where she spent the large part of the following years working on poems, essays and short stories as well as opera [libretti](/wiki/Libretto "Libretto") in collaboration with [Hans Werner Henze](/wiki/Hans_Werner_Henze "Hans Werner Henze"), which soon brought with them international fame and numerous awards.

### Writings

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Writings")]

Bachmann's doctoral dissertation expresses her growing disillusionment with [Heideggerian](/wiki/Heideggerian "Heideggerian") [existentialism](/wiki/Existentialism "Existentialism"), which was in part resolved through her growing interest in [Ludwig Wittgenstein](/wiki/Ludwig_Wittgenstein "Ludwig Wittgenstein"), whose *[Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus](/wiki/Tractatus_Logico-Philosophicus "Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus")* significantly influenced her relationship to language.[[9]](#cite_note-9) During her lifetime, Bachmann was known mostly for her two collections of poetry, *Die gestundete Zeit* (Time Deferred) and *Anrufung des Grossen Bären* (Invocation of Ursa Major).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Bachmann's literary work focuses on themes like [personal boundaries](/wiki/Personal_boundaries "Personal boundaries"), establishment of the truth, and [philosophy of language](/wiki/Philosophy_of_language "Philosophy of language"), the latter in the tradition of Wittgenstein. Many of her prose works represent the struggles of women to survive and to find a voice in post-war society. She also addresses the histories of [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") and [fascism](/wiki/Fascism "Fascism"), in particular, the persistence of imperialist ideas in the present.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Fascism was a recurring theme in her writings. In her novel *Der Fall Franza* (*The Case of Franza*) Bachmann argued that fascism had not died in 1945 but had survived in the German speaking world of the 1960s in human relations and particularly in men's oppression of women. In Germany the achievements of the [women's rights](/wiki/Women%27s_rights "Women's rights") campaign at the end of the 19th and beginning of the 20th century had been systematically undone by the fascist [Nazi regime](/wiki/Nazi_regime "Nazi regime") in the 1930s. Bachmann's engagement with fascism followed that of other women writers who in the immediate post-war period dealt with fascism from a woman's perspective, such as [Anna Seghers](/wiki/Anna_Seghers "Anna Seghers"), [Ilse Aichinger](/wiki/Ilse_Aichinger "Ilse Aichinger"), [Ingeborg Drewitz](/wiki/Ingeborg_Drewitz "Ingeborg Drewitz") and [Christa Wolf](/wiki/Christa_Wolf "Christa Wolf").[[12]](#cite_note-12)

A crisis of *[Vergangenheitsbewältigung](/wiki/Vergangenheitsbew%C3%A4ltigung "Vergangenheitsbewältigung")*, along with the fear of the continued existence of [National Socialism](/wiki/National_Socialism "National Socialism") within democracy, suffuses Bachmann's oeuvre. In her work for radio, this takes the form of a self-conscious pivoting between the possibility of freedom and the inevitability of imprisonment. Her first radio play *Ein Geschäft mit Träumen* (*A Shop for Dreams*) is concerned with the inhumanity of violence and oppression. *Der gute Gott von Manhattan* (*The Good God of Manhattan*) consciously echoes [Bertolt Brecht](/wiki/Bertolt_Brecht "Bertolt Brecht")'s *[The Good Person of Szechwan](/wiki/The_Good_Person_of_Szechwan "The Good Person of Szechwan")*, as it tackles the impossibility of Good and Love surviving in capitalist, consumerist societies. In her analysis of Bachmann's radio drama *Die Zikaden* (*The Cicadas*), which was written in [Ischia](/wiki/Ischia "Ischia") and then [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") towards the end of 1954, and first broadcast on [Nordwestdeutscher Rundfunk](/wiki/Nordwestdeutscher_Rundfunk "Nordwestdeutscher Rundfunk") (NWDR) on 25 March 1955, Lucy Jeffery states that

> The transitory existence of the exiled or marginalised writer who escapes prejudice, conflict, and dominance is paralleled by the experience of the refugee. The feeling of unsettledness is measured against the desire to find that utopian land away (both geographically and temporally) from suffering. Yet, as Bachmann knows too well, escapism is a temporary [heterotopia](/wiki/Heterotopia_(space) "Heterotopia (space)") where guilt and longing cannot be kept at bay.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Similar themes can also be found throughout Bachmann's writings in works such as *Ein Wildermuth* (*A Wildermuth*), included in *Das dreißigste Jahr* (*The Thirtieth Year: Stories*, published in 1961), *[Malina](/wiki/Malina_(novel) "Malina (novel)")* (published in 1971), and *Kriegstagebuch* (*War Diary*, published posthumously in 2010).

Bachmann was also in the vanguard of Austrian women writers who discovered in their private lives the political realities from which they attempted to achieve emancipation. Bachmann's writings and those of [Barbara Frischmuth](/wiki/Barbara_Frischmuth "Barbara Frischmuth"), [Brigitte Schwaiger](/wiki/Brigitte_Schwaiger "Brigitte Schwaiger") and [Anna Mitgutsch](/wiki/Anna_Mitgutsch "Anna Mitgutsch") were widely published in Germany. Male Austrian authors such as [Franz Innerhofer](/w/index.php?title=Franz_Innerhofer&action=edit&redlink=1 "Franz Innerhofer (page does not exist)"), [Josef Winkler](/wiki/Josef_Winkler_(writer) "Josef Winkler (writer)") and [Peter Turrini](/wiki/Peter_Turrini "Peter Turrini") wrote equally popular works on traumatic experiences of socialisation. Often these authors produced their works for major German publishing houses. After Bachmann's death in 1973, Austrian writers such as [Thomas Bernhard](/wiki/Thomas_Bernhard "Thomas Bernhard"), [Peter Handke](/wiki/Peter_Handke "Peter Handke") and [Elfriede Jelinek](/wiki/Elfriede_Jelinek "Elfriede Jelinek") continued the tradition of Austrian literature in Germany.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Lectures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Lectures")]

Between November 1959 and February 1960 Bachmann gave five lectures on poetics at the [Goethe University Frankfurt](/wiki/Goethe_University_Frankfurt "Goethe University Frankfurt"). Known as the *Frankfurter Vorlesungen: Probleme zeitgenössischer Dichtung* (*Frankfurt Lectures: Problems of Contemporary Writings*) they are historically and substantively Bachmann's central work. In it she explained recurring themes in her early literary publications and she discussed the function of literature in society.[[15]](#cite_note-Achberger1-15) Bachmann insisted that literature had to be viewed in its historic context, thus foreshadowing a rising interest in studying the connection between literary discourse and the contemporary understanding of history.[[15]](#cite_note-Achberger1-15)

In the first lecture on *Fragen und Scheinfragen* (Questions and Pseudo-Questions) Bachmann focused on the role of writers in the post-war society and listed some essential questions that she defines "destructive and frightening in their simplicity". They are: why write? What do we mean by change and why do we want it through art? What are the limitations of the writer who wants to bring about change? According to Karen Achberger

> Bachmann views the great literary accomplishments of the twentieth century as expressions in language and poetic form of a moral and intellectual renewal in the individual writers; it is the writer's new thinking and experiencing that forms the core of their literary works, and lets them come closer to a new language. (…) Bachmann stresses the need for a new language inhabited by a new spirit. (…) She also associates literary renewal with writers on the verge of silence due to self-doubt and despair over the impotence of language and she cites in this context [Hofmannsthal](/wiki/Hugo_von_Hofmannsthal "Hugo von Hofmannsthal")'s *[Ein Brief](/wiki/The_Lord_Chandos_Letter "The Lord Chandos Letter")* (1902) … as the first articulation of this dilemma.[[15]](#cite_note-Achberger1-15)

In the second lecture, *Über Gedichte* (On Poetry), she distinguished poetry with its new power to grasp reality in its language, from other genres such as novels and plays. With reference to [Günter Eich](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Eich "Günter Eich") and [Stefan George](/wiki/Stefan_George "Stefan George") she identified a new generation of "poet-prophets" whose mission consisted in leading the world to the discovery of an "ever purer heaven of art" (George). Bachmann set these poets apart from the [Surrealists](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism") who aspired to violence and the [Futurists](/wiki/Futurism "Futurism") who claimed that "war is beautiful". She argued that these two movements exemplified *[art for art's sake](/wiki/Art_for_art%27s_sake "Art for art's sake")* and that the careers of [Gottfried Benn](/wiki/Gottfried_Benn "Gottfried Benn") and [Ezra Pound](/wiki/Ezra_Pound "Ezra Pound") exemplified the "easy friendship between pure aestheticism with political barbarism" (Achberger). She referenced [Kafka](/wiki/Kafka "Kafka") on the need (with his words) to "take the axe to the frozen sea in us" and refuse to remain indifferent to the injustices that are perpetrated before our eyes. In the lecture she also named writings of [Nelly Sachs](/wiki/Nelly_Sachs "Nelly Sachs"), [Marie Luise Kaschnitz](/wiki/Marie_Luise_Kaschnitz "Marie Luise Kaschnitz"), [Hans Magnus Enzensberger](/wiki/Hans_Magnus_Enzensberger "Hans Magnus Enzensberger") and [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan") as examples of his concept of new poetry.[[16]](#cite_note-Achberger2-16)

In the third lecture, on *Das schreibende Ich* (The writing *I*), Bachmann addressed the question of the [first-person narrator](/wiki/First-person_narrator "First-person narrator"). She was concerned with the "accountability and authority, the authenticity and reliability, of the person in the position of narrating the work" (Achberger). She distinguished between the unproblematic "I" in letters and diaries, which conceals the person from the author, and the unproblematic "I" in memoirs, in which a "'naive' handling of the first person is requested (Achberger). She argued that [Henry Miller](/wiki/Henry_Miller "Henry Miller") and [Céline](/wiki/Louis-Ferdinand_C%C3%A9line "Louis-Ferdinand Céline") placed "themselves and their personal experience directly at the centre of their novels" (Achberger). She referenced [Tolstoy](/wiki/Tolstoy "Tolstoy")'s *[The Kreutzer Sonata](/wiki/The_Kreutzer_Sonata "The Kreutzer Sonata")* and [Dostoyevsky](/wiki/Dostoyevsky "Dostoyevsky")'s *[The House of Dead](/wiki/The_House_of_the_Dead_(novel) "The House of the Dead (novel)")* as first-person narrators of the inner story. She also argued that narrators could provide a new treatment of time (for example [Italo Svevo](/wiki/Italo_Svevo "Italo Svevo")), of material (for example [Proust](/wiki/Proust "Proust")) or of space (for example [Hans Henny Jahnn](/wiki/Hans_Henny_Jahnn "Hans Henny Jahnn")). According to Bachmann, in the modern novel the "I" had "shifted: the narrator no longer lives in the story, but rather, the story lives inside the narrator" (Achberger).[[16]](#cite_note-Achberger2-16)

In the fourth lecture, *Der Umgang mit Namen* (The close association with names), Bachmann explored how names could have a life of their own. She discussed the use of names in contemporary literature. She identified "denied names" such as in Kafka's *[The Castle](/wiki/The_Castle_(novel) "The Castle (novel)")*, "ironic naming" by [Thomas Mann](/wiki/Thomas_Mann "Thomas Mann"), "name games" in [Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce")'s *[Ulysses](/wiki/Ulysses_(novel) "Ulysses (novel)")* and instances where the identity of the character is not secured by a name but by the context, such as in [Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner")'s *[The Sound and the Fury](/wiki/The_Sound_and_the_Fury "The Sound and the Fury")*.[[17]](#cite_note-Achberger3-17)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/de/Klagenfurt_-_Musilhaus_-_Ingeborg_Bachmann.jpg/250px-Klagenfurt_-_Musilhaus_-_Ingeborg_Bachmann.jpg)](/wiki/File:Klagenfurt_-_Musilhaus_-_Ingeborg_Bachmann.jpg)

Graffiti portrait of Bachmann at the [Robert Musil](/wiki/Robert_Musil "Robert Musil") Museum in [Klagenfurt](/wiki/Klagenfurt "Klagenfurt").

In the fifth lecture on *Literatur als Utopie* (Literature as Utopia), she turned to the question of what makes literature [utopian](/wiki/Utopian "Utopian"). She argued that it was the process that was set in motion in the writer and reader, as a result of their interaction with literature, which made a work utopian. She argued that literature could make us aware of a lack, both in the work and in our own world. Readers could remove this lack by giving the work a chance in our time. Thus she argued that each work of literature is "a realm which reaches forward and has unknown limits".[[15]](#cite_note-Achberger1-15) Bachmann's understanding of utopia as a direction rather than a goal, and her argument that it was the function of literature to take an utopian direction, stemmed from [Robert Musil](/wiki/Robert_Musil "Robert Musil"), who had analysed European modernism in his 1908 dissertation on [Ernst Mach](/wiki/Ernst_Mach "Ernst Mach"), *Beitrag zur Beurteilung der Lehren Machs* (Contribution to the assessment of Mach's theories.[[17]](#cite_note-Achberger3-17)

## Later life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Later life and death")]

During her later years she suffered from alcoholism and from an addiction to medication ([barbiturates](/wiki/Barbiturate "Barbiturate") and [benzodiazepines](/wiki/Benzodiazepines "Benzodiazepines")) prescribed by her doctor.[[18]](#cite_note-18) A friend described it:

> "I was deeply shocked by the magnitude of her tablet addiction. It must have been 100 per day, the bin was full of empty boxes. She looked bad, she was waxlike and pale. And her whole body was covered in bruises. I wondered what could have caused them. Then, when I saw how she slipped her [Gauloise](/wiki/Gauloises "Gauloises") that she smoked and let it burn off on her arm, I realized: burns caused by falling cigarettes. The numerous tablets had made her body insensible to pain."[[19]](#cite_note-19)

On the night of 25 September 1973, her nightgown caught fire and she was taken to the [Sant'Eugenio Hospital](/wiki/Sant%27Eugenio_Hospital "Sant'Eugenio Hospital") at 7:05 A.M. the following morning for treatment of second and third degree burns.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Local police concluded that the fire was caused by a cigarette. During her stay, she experienced withdrawal symptoms from [barbiturate](/wiki/Barbiturate "Barbiturate") [substance abuse](/wiki/Substance_abuse "Substance abuse"), though the doctors treating her were not aware of the cause. This may have contributed to her subsequent death on 17 October 1973.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Although German language writers such as [Hilde Domin](/wiki/Hilde_Domin "Hilde Domin"), [Luise Rinser](/wiki/Luise_Rinser "Luise Rinser") and [Nelly Sachs](/wiki/Nelly_Sachs "Nelly Sachs") had published notable works on women's issues in the post-war period, it was only in the 1970s that a [feminist](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism") movement emerged in [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany").[[23]](#cite_note-23) After her death, Bachmann became popular among feminist readers. Feminist scholars' engagement with her work after her death led to a wave of scholarship that also drew attention to her prose work.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Her works gained popularity within the emerging *Frauenliteratur* (*women's literature*) movement which struggled to find the authentic female voice. New publishing houses carried the movement, such as the feminist press *Frauenoffensive* (*Women's Offensive*), which published writings by [Verena Stefan](/wiki/Verena_Stefan "Verena Stefan").[[25]](#cite_note-25)

In 2021, her childhood home on Henselstraße in Klagenfurt was purchased by the state of [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia "Carinthia"), to be turned into a museum dedicated to her.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### In film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: In film")]

*[The Dreamed Ones](/w/index.php?title=The_Dreamed_Ones&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Dreamed Ones (page does not exist)")* (*Die Geträumten*; 2016), is a feature film based on the almost 20-year correspondence between Bachmann and poet [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan").[[31]](#cite_note-31) It was directed by [Ruth Beckermann](/wiki/Ruth_Beckermann "Ruth Beckermann") and won the SCAM International Award at [Cinéma du Réel](/wiki/Cin%C3%A9ma_du_R%C3%A9el "Cinéma du Réel") 2016.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

A biographical film, *[Ingeborg Bachmann – Journey into the Desert](/wiki/Ingeborg_Bachmann_%E2%80%93_Journey_into_the_Desert "Ingeborg Bachmann – Journey into the Desert")*, starring [Vicky Krieps](/wiki/Vicky_Krieps "Vicky Krieps") as Bachmann and directed by [Margarethe von Trotta](/wiki/Margarethe_von_Trotta "Margarethe von Trotta"), premiered at [73rd Berlin International Film Festival](/wiki/73rd_Berlin_International_Film_Festival "73rd Berlin International Film Festival") in February 2023, and gets a cinema release on 26 October 2023.[[33]](#cite_note-poly-33) The film focuses on her relationship with [Max Frisch](/wiki/Max_Frisch "Max Frisch") and the impact it had on her life. It also depicts her friends, composer [Hans Werner Henze](/wiki/Hans_Werner_Henze "Hans Werner Henze"), and writer [Adolf Opel](/wiki/Adolf_Opel "Adolf Opel"), with whom she travelled to Egypt to experience the desert.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ingeborg_Bachmann&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Personal life")]

From 1945 to 1946, Bachmann fell in love with a former member of the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), the Viennese Jew [Jack Hamesh](/w/index.php?title=Jake_Hamesh&action=edit&redlink=1 "Jake Hamesh (page does not exist)").[[35]](#cite_note-35) In May 1948 she began in Vienna a love affair with the poet and Holocaust survivor [Paul Celan](/wiki/Paul_Celan "Paul Celan").[[36]](#cite_note-36) In 1955 she met the political scientist [Henry Kissinger](/wiki/Henry_Kissinger "Henry Kissinger"); even though he was married and had two children, the two had a romantic relationship that lasted several years.[[37]](#cite_note-37) From 1958 to 1963, she lived on and off with Swiss playwright [Max Frisch](/wiki/Max_Frisch "Max Frisch"). Her 1971 novel, *[Malina](/wiki/Malina_(novel) "Malina (novel)")*, has been described as a response, at least partially, to his 1964 novel *[Mein Name sei Gantenbein](/wiki/Gantenbein "Gantenbein")*.[[38]](#cite_note-Revesz-2005-38) She never married nor had children.
